You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 55 actions; a strong score needs about 21 or fewer. You are 2.6x over the target.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after the MOUSE click. Let me check what changed by comparing the frames. The agent seems to have moved from the blue platform to the left side. Let me inspect the current state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after MOUSE click
# Compare agent position before and after
prev_nodes = previous_frame.segmentation['nodes']
curr_nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

print("=== Before ===")
for n in prev_nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('Y', 'b'):
        print(f"{n['color']}: id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print("=== After ===")
for n in curr_nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('Y', 'b'):
        print(f"{n['color']}: id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check the blue blocks - did they change?
prev_blue = [n for n in prev_nodes if n['color'] == 'b']
curr_blue = [n for n in curr_nodes if n['color'] == 'b']
print(f"\nBlue blocks before: {len(prev_blue)}, after: {len(curr_blue)}")

# Check agent position
prev_agent = [n for n in prev_nodes if n['color'] == 'Y']
curr_agent = [n for n in curr_nodes if n['color'] == 'Y']
print(f"Agent before: {[n['boundary'] for n in prev_agent]}")
print(f"Agent after: {[n['boundary'] for n in curr_agent]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The agent and blue blocks are in the same position. But the board_changed was True. Let me check what actually changed - maybe it's the green blocks or some other element. Let me look at the full segmentation diff.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed - compare all nodes
prev_nodes = previous_frame.segmentation['nodes']
curr_nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']

print(f"Before: {len(prev_nodes)} nodes, After: {len(curr_nodes)} nodes")

# Compare by color and hash
prev_by_color = {}
for n in prev_nodes:
    prev_by_color.setdefault(n['color'], []).append(n)
    
curr_by_color = {}
for n in curr_nodes:
    curr_by_color.setdefault(n['color'], []).append(n)

print("\nColor counts before:", {k: len(v) for k, v in prev_by_color.items()})
print("Color counts after:", {k: len(v) for k, v in curr_by_color.items()})

# Check for differences in node hashes
prev_hashes = {(n['color'], n['hash']) for n in prev_nodes}
curr_hashes = {(n['color'], n['hash']) for n in curr_nodes}
print(f"\nHash diff: {prev_hashes.symmetric_difference(curr_hashes)}")

# Check green blocks (N) specifically
prev_green = [n for n in prev_nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
curr_green = [n for n in curr_nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"\nGreen blocks: before={len(prev_green)}, after={len(curr_green)}")
for i, (p, c) in enumerate(zip(prev_green, curr_green)):
    if p['boundary'] != c['boundary']:
        print(f"  Green {i}: before={p['boundary']}, after={c['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
THINKING
sections.content:
The hashes changed for 'W' (white) and 'p' (purple) objects. The purple objects are at the bottom - they're the floor. The white object might be a HUD element. The gameplay objects (green blocks, blue blocks, agent) are all unchanged.
So the MOUSE click didn't actually do anything meaningful to the gameplay. The board_changed flag was True but only due to minor HUD/timer changes.
Let me think about this differently. The agent is on the left platform, and there are green blocks on the right. The blue blocks block the path. Maybe I need to:
1. Move the agent to a different position first
2. Or click on the green blocks instead
3. Or the blue blocks need to be clicked at a different position
Let me try clicking on the green blocks to see if they disappear or change.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The MOUSE click didn't change gameplay objects, only HUD
# Let me try clicking on green blocks instead
# Green blocks are at various positions - let me find one on the right side

nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
green_blocks = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']

# Find green blocks on the right side (col > 30)
right_green = [n for n in green_blocks if n['boundary'][0][1] > 30]
print(f"Right-side green blocks: {len(right_green)}")
for n in right_green:
    print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}, center~=[{(n['boundary'][0][0]+n['boundary'][4][0])//2}, {(n['boundary'][0][1]+n['boundary'][4][1])//2}]")

# Try clicking on one of the right-side green blocks
if right_green:
    g = right_green[0]
    # Estimate center from boundary
    rows = [p[0] for p in g['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in g['boundary']]
    center_r = (min(rows) + max(rows)) // 2
    center_c = (min(cols) + max(cols)) // 2
    print(f"\nClicking at row={center_r}, col={center_c}")
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center_r, 'col': center_c}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
